role_summary: Educator and civil rights organizer; founder of the Highlander Folk School Citizenship Schools (1957), which trained thousands of Black Southerners in literacy and civic preparation; the curriculum was adopted by SCLC and run by Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29).
ai_reading: Four corpus voices discuss Clark: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29, who directed the Citizenship Education Program Clark founded), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC Northern support), the Carawan joint interview (entry 11, Highlander music teachers), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96, Mississippi). The smallest external-figure footprint in the catalog, but the four voices that do discuss her are exactly the ones whose own work most directly inherited from Clark's pedagogical framework: Cotton ran the program, the Carawans taught at Highlander, Garman Robinson organized at SNCC's Atlanta office (which used Clark's materials), Connor was a Mississippi field organizer (whose voter-registration work Clark's citizenship curriculum supported).
biographical_paragraph: Septima Clark is discussed in this corpus by 4 interviewees across the catalog (Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7), Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (entry 11), Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Septima Clark as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

sources.title: Cynthia Stokes Brown (ed.), Ready From Within: Septima Clark and the Civil Rights Movement
